Now I have all the information needed. Let me present the Theme Selection Wizard as required by the local frontend steering file.

---

**🎨 Theme Selection Wizard**

Before I generate the frontend, which visual theme should I use for the LLM GPU Sizer?

Pick a number (1–20):

| # | Theme | Vibe |
|---|-------|------|
| 1 | **Neubrutalism** | Bold colors, thick black borders, hard offset shadows |
| 2 | **Kinetic Typography** | Giant text IS the design — words overlap, rotate, dominate |
| 3 | **Sketchy Wireframe** | Hand-drawn wobbly lines, gray/white, napkin-sketch feel |
| 4 | **Aurora UI** | Ethereal gradient mesh, northern-lights glow, glassmorphism |
| 5 | **Bento Grid** | Japanese bento-box modular tiles, asymmetric layout |
| 6 | **Anti-Design** | Punk rock chaos — clashing colors, broken grids |
| 7 | **Dark Precision SaaS** | Linear/Vercel vibe — near-black, razor-sharp, restrained |
| 8 | **Dark Maximalism** | Dark mode meets baroque — gold accents, ornate textures |
| 9 | **Liquid Motion** | Fluid morphing blobs, organic curves, living UI |
| 10 | **Monochrome Luxe** | Single-color palette, editorial typography, pure elegance |
| 11 | **Cyberpunk HUD** | Neon glitch, scan lines, sci-fi tactical interface |
| 12 | **Organic Minimalism** | Earth tones, nature curves, ceramics studio calm |
| 13 | **Collage Punk** | Zine aesthetic — torn textures, stamps, scissors-and-glue |
| 14 | **Swiss Clean** | Strict grid, Helvetica worldview, form follows function |
| 15 | **High-Contrast Duotone** | Two colors max, bold readability, accessibility-first |
| 16 | **Grain & Texture Analog** | Film grain, vintage tones, analog warmth |
| 17 | **Blueprint Wireframe** | Technical drawing grid, annotation labels, architecture UI |
| 18 | **Editorial Serif** | Vogue magazine meets dashboard — serif, pull-quotes |
| 19 | **MySpace / Geocities** | 1999 homepage chaos — neon links, tiled backgrounds |
| 20 | **shadcn Default** | Clean zinc grays, subtle borders, polished dev defaults |

Which theme number would you like?

Done! Here's what was created:

## Project Structure

```
/home/ec2-user/llm-sizer/
├── index.html                          # Static LLM GPU Sizer (Aurora UI theme)
├── LLM GPU Sizing Guide.md
└── infrastructure/                     # CDK project (ap-southeast-7)
    ├── bin/infrastructure.ts           # Stack entry point
    └── lib/infrastructure-stack.ts     # S3 + CloudFront stack
```

## Static Site (`index.html`)

- **Aurora UI theme**: dark base with ethereal gradient mesh backgrounds, glassmorphism cards, northern-lights animation
- **Inputs**: Model parameters (B), precision (FP32/FP16/INT8/INT4), context length (4K–32K), batch size
- **Outputs**: Model weights memory, KV cache memory, total GPU memory required
- **Recommendation**: Best-fit AWS GPU instance (G5 / P4d / P5) with throughput & cost info, tensor parallelism indicator

## CDK Infrastructure (iac-security compliant)

- ✅ S3 bucket with `BlockPublicAccess.BLOCK_ALL` + S3-managed encryption
- ✅ CloudFront distribution with **Origin Access Control (OAC)** — no public S3 access
- ✅ HTTPS redirect enforced
- ✅ Auto-deploys `index.html` to S3 on `cdk deploy`
- ✅ Targets `ap-southeast-7`

## Deploy

```bash
cd /home/ec2-user/llm-sizer/infrastructure
npx cdk deploy
```

The CloudFront URL will be output after deployment.

Done. To confirm the architecture:

**Security check ✅** — The stack does NOT use S3 static website hosting (which would require public bucket). Instead it uses:
- Private S3 bucket (all public access blocked + encrypted)
- CloudFront with Origin Access Control (OAC) to read from S3 securely

**Deploy:**
```bash
./deploy.sh
```

The script installs deps, synthesizes, and deploys in one shot with `--require-approval never` for quick production deployment.

✅ **Deployed successfully!**

Your LLM GPU Sizer is live at: **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**

Total deployment time was ~4.5 minutes (most of that was CloudFront distribution creation). The site is served securely via HTTPS through CloudFront with the S3 bucket remaining fully private.

- DeepSeek V3.2
- Google (Gemma 4 31B, Gemma 3 27B/12B/4B)
- Qwen (235B, 32B, Next 80B)
- NVIDIA (Nemotron 3 Super 120B, Nemotron Nano 2)
- Moonshot (Kimi K2)
- MiniMax (M2.5)

**2. Self-Host GPU Instances Table** — 8 instance types with monthly costs:
- g5.xlarge ($686/mo) through p5.48xlarge ($71,773/mo)

**3. Break-Even Calculator** — Select any Bedrock model + any instance, pick your I/O ratio, and it calculates exactly how many million tokens/month you need before self-hosting becomes cheaper.

✅ Updated and deployed at **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**

Changes to the Break-Even Calculator:

1. **Slider for I/O ratio** — Drag from 0% to 100% input (with live label showing e.g. "75% input / 25% output"). Much easier than a dropdown.

2. **Recommended instance auto-selected per model** — When you pick a Bedrock model, the Self-Host Instance dropdown automatically selects the best-fit GPU instance (with a brief ⭐ badge). The mapping:
   - ≤13B models → g5.xlarge
   - ~24-30B models → g6e.xlarge (L40S 48GB)

   - 100-235B models → p4d.24xlarge (8× A100)
   - 400B+ / MoE models → p5.48xlarge (8× H100)

✅ Deployed! Live at **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**

Here's what changed:

**Aurora Green Theme:**
- Deep emerald/green color palette (#10b981, #059669, #6ee7b7, #34d399)
- Three animated aurora orbs in green tones (instead of purple/blue)
- Green gradient mesh background with organic motion
- All accent colors, glows, borders, and hover states now green

**Bootstrap Icons:**
- Added throughout — GPU card, CPU, calculator, server, memory, lightning, graphs, etc.
- Icons in card headers, labels, table headers, metric boxes, and recommendation items

**Improved Layout:**
- Dedicated header section with large GPU icon
- Card headers with icon + title pattern
- Table wrappers with proper overflow handling and rounded borders
- Better spacing, larger touch targets
- Fade-in animation on tab switch
- Footer with branding
- Stronger visual hierarchy with uppercase labels, letter-spacing, and weight contrast

✅ Done! Quick deploy took just a few seconds vs ~2 minutes for full CDK.

**Changes made:**

1. **Pink aurora accent color** — Added a second accent with pink orbs (`#ec4899`, `#f9a8d4`) in the background. The h1 title gradient now flows from green → emerald → pink. Two pink aurora orbs animate alongside the green ones.

2. **Favicon** — Inline SVG favicon with a ⚡ lightning bolt on dark background (no external file needed).

3. **deploy.sh updated** with two modes:
   - `./deploy.sh` — Full CDK deploy (infra + code, ~2 min)
   - `./deploy.sh --quick` or `./deploy.sh -q` — Frontend only: syncs files to S3 + invalidates CloudFront cache (~5 seconds)

Live at: **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**

✅ Done — reverted to the original **Aurora UI** theme (indigo/purple/cyan) with the pink accent orb retained. Live at **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**

The color scheme is now back to:
- Primary: indigo `#6366F1` / purple `#8b5cf6`
- Secondary aurora: cyan `#06b6d4`
- Pink accent orb: `#ec4899` (kept as requested second accent)
- Dark background: `#0a0a1a`
- Text: cool slate tones

✅ Deployed. The page now pops more with:

- **Metric values** — gradient text (indigo → cyan) instead of flat color
- **Metric icons** — pink accent color for visual contrast
- **Metric boxes** — subtle glow shadow
- **Recommendation values** — brighter lavender (`#c7d2fe`)
- **Recommendation icons** — pink accent
- **Table monthly costs** — green with text glow effect
- **Break-even point** — orange with glow (`#fb923c`) for maximum attention
- **Warning values** — yellow with text-shadow glow

Live at **https://d2ij9ccmpc9jvp.cloudfront.net**
Done. Added **g7e.xlarge** (1× L40S Ada, 48 GB, $1.95/hr) and **g7e.12xlarge** (4× L40S Ada, 192 GB, $11.09/hr) to both the instance table and the break-even calculator dropdown.
Done. Added **g7.xlarge** (1× B200, 96 GB, $2.85/hr) and **g7.12xlarge** (4× B200, 384 GB, $16.20/hr) to the instance list.

Done. The Break-Even Calculator now shows a **Memory Check** after results:

- **⚠️ Red warning** — if the instance doesn't have enough VRAM for the model (shows deficit in GB and suggests quantization or larger instance)
- **✅ Green OK** — if memory fits (shows headroom available)

It estimates VRAM needed using FP16 weights × 1.6 overhead factor, and for MoE models it uses ~25% active parameters. For example, selecting Llama 3.3 70B with a g5.xlarge (24 GB) will show a clear deficit warning.

Done. The Break-Even Calculator now includes **Precision**, **Context Length**, and **Batch Size** inputs, and the memory check uses them for accurate estimation:

- Memory breakdown shows: `Weights: X GB + KV Cache: Y GB + Overhead: Z GB = Total GB`
- KV Cache scales with context length and batch size (same formula as Tab 1)
- Selecting INT4 instead of FP16 will show 75% less memory needed
- Larger context (128K) or batch (32+) will properly inflate the KV cache estimate and trigger warnings on smaller instances
Done. The **Break-Even Calculator** is now the first card in the "Bedrock vs Self-Host" tab, followed by the pricing reference tables below it.
✅ Done! Repo created and pushed to **https://github.com/WarotAsawa/llm-sizer**

content.data:
# LLM GPU Sizing Guide: Parameters, Throughput & I/O

**Version**: 2026-06-22 | **Based on**: AWS Prescriptive Guidance + Industry Benchmarks

---

## 1. GPU Memory Requirements Formula

### A. Model Weights Memory (Dominant Cost)

**Formula:**
```
Model Weights (GB) = Parameters (Billions) × Bytes per Parameter
```

**Precision Levels & Their Impact:**

| Precision | Bits | Bytes/Param | 7B Model | 13B Model | 70B Model |
|-----------|------|------------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **FP32** (Full) | 32 | 4 | 28 GB | 52 GB | 280 GB |
| **FP16** (Half) | 16 | 2 | 14 GB | 26 GB | 140 GB ⭐ |
| **INT8** (Quantized) | 8 | 1 | 7 GB | 13 GB | 70 GB |
| **INT4** (Compressed) | 4 | 0.5 | 3.5 GB | 6.5 GB | 35 GB |

**Key Insight:** Quantization from FP16 → INT4 saves 75% memory with <1-2% accuracy loss.

---

### B. KV Cache (Key-Value Cache) — Attention Memory

The KV cache stores processed input/output tokens across all transformer attention layers.

**Formula (Approximate):**
```
KV Cache (GB) ≈ 2 × Context_Length × Batch_Size × Num_Heads × Bytes_Per_Token
```

**Rule of Thumb:**
- **Baseline**: KV cache ≈ **50% of model weights** for typical workloads
- **Long context** (8K-32K tokens): Can exceed model weights
- **High concurrency** (batch size > 32): KV cache dominates memory

**Scaling Factors:**

| Factor | Impact | Example |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Context Length** | Linear | 4K → 8K = 2× KV cache |
| **Batch Size** | Linear | Batch 4 → 16 = 4× KV cache |
| **Attention Heads** | Linear | 32 → 128 heads = 4× KV cache |
| **Precision** | Linear | FP16 → INT8 = 50% reduction |

---

### C. Total GPU Memory Breakdown

**Typical Distribution for Inference:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Total GPU Memory Budget                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Model Weights         70-75% ████████  │
│  KV Cache              15-20% ██        │
│  Runtime Overhead       5-10% █         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Runtime Overhead Includes:**
- CUDA context initialization
- Activation tensors during forward pass
- Framework buffers (PyTorch, vLLM, etc.)

**Total Formula:**
```
Total Memory Needed = Model Weights × (1 + KV_Cache_Factor + Overhead_Factor)
                    ≈ Model Weights × 1.6  (for typical small batch inference)
```

---

## 2. AWS Reference: Minimum GPU Memory by Model Size

**Source:** [AWS Prescriptive Guidance - Right-sizing an Inference System](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/gen-ai-inference-architecture-and-best-practices-on-aws/right-sizing-and-auto-scaling.html)

| Model Size | FP16 (2-byte) | INT8 (1-byte) | INT4 (0.5-byte) | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|--------------------|-------|
| **7B** | 14 GB | 7 GB | 3.5 GB | Fits A10G with headroom |
| **13B** | 26 GB | 13 GB | 6.5 GB | Fits A100-40GB |
| **70B** | 140 GB | 70 GB | 35 GB | Requires tensor parallelism ↓ |

**Add 20-50% margin** for KV cache + runtime overhead.

**Practical Examples:**
- **7B FP16 (14 GB)** → Use A100-40GB or A10G-24GB with INT4
- **13B FP16 (26 GB)** → Use A100-40GB or A100-80GB
- **70B FP16 (140 GB)** → Use P4d (8× A100-80GB) or P5 (8× H100-80GB) with tensor parallelism

---

## 3. Token Throughput & Compute Requirements

### A. Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Why It Matters |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **RPS** (Requests/Sec) | Number of inference requests per second | Measures throughput capacity |
| **Tokens/Sec** | Total output tokens generated per second | Direct cost driver |
| **TTFT** (Time to First Token) | Latency from request to first output token | User-facing latency |
| **Tokens Generated** | Avg output tokens per request | Affects total cost & latency |

**Relationships:**
```
Tokens/Sec = RPS × Avg_Output_Tokens_Per_Request
```

---

### B. EC2 GPU Instance Throughput Benchmarks (2026)

**8B Model Inference:**

| Instance Type | GPU | GPU Memory | Throughput | Cost per 1M tokens | Use Case |
|---------------|-----|-----------|------------|-------------------|----------|
| **G5.xlarge** | A10G | 24 GB | ~350 tok/s | $1.50 | Cost-optimized |
| **P4d.24xlarge** | 8× A100-80GB | 640 GB | ~3,800 tok/s | $0.85 | High throughput |
| **P5.48xlarge** | 8× H100-80GB | 640 GB | ~7,000 tok/s | $2.02 | Latency-critical |

**70B Model Inference (with tensor parallelism):**

| Instance Type | Throughput | TTFT | Cost/1M tokens | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P4d.24xlarge** | ~400 tok/s | ~50-80ms | $0.90 | Cost-optimized for 70B |
| **P5.48xlarge** | ~4,500-6,500 tok/s | ~20-30ms | $2.50 | High throughput |

**Key Insight:**
- **G5 is best for cost** (smaller models ≤ 13B)
- **P4d is best for cost/performance** (70B models)
- **P5 is best for latency** (latency-critical apps)

---

### C. Estimating Required Instances

**Formula:**
```
Required Instances = Target_Throughput_Tokens_Per_Sec / Per_Instance_Throughput
```

**Example 1: Small model, moderate throughput**
```
Use case: 8B model, need 1,000 tokens/sec
  → G5.xlarge throughput: 350 tok/s
  → Instances needed: 1000 / 350 = 2.9 → 3 instances
  → Cost: 3 × $0.94/hr = $2.82/hr
```

**Example 2: Large model, high throughput**
```
Use case: 70B model, need 2,000 tokens/sec
  → P4d.24xlarge throughput: 400 tok/s
  → Instances needed: 2000 / 400 = 5 instances
  → Cost: 5 × $12.48/hr = $62.40/hr (or $2.02/M tokens)
```

**Example 3: Large model, very high throughput**
```
Use case: 70B model, need 5,000 tokens/sec
  → P4d.24xlarge: need 13 instances = $162/hr
  → P5.48xlarge: need 1 instance = $98/hr ✓ (better value for high throughput)
```

---

## 4. Input/Output (I/O) Considerations

### A. Context Window Impact

**How Context Length Affects GPU Requirements:**

| Context Length | KV Cache Factor | Example (70B model) |
|---|---|---|
| 4K (short) | ~50% of weights | 140GB weights + 70GB KV = 210GB |
| 8K (medium) | ~100% of weights | 140GB weights + 140GB KV = 280GB |
| 32K (long) | ~400% of weights | 140GB weights + 560GB KV = 700GB |

**Impact on Throughput:**
- Longer context = more attention computations
- **Throughput degrades** ~linearly with context length
- 4K context might get 400 tok/s, but 32K context might drop to 100 tok/s

**Implication:** Long context (32K+) workloads require larger instances or more inference nodes.

---

### B. Batch Size & Concurrency

**Batch Size**: Number of concurrent requests processed on the same GPU at once.

| Batch Size | Memory Impact | KV Cache Growth | Throughput | Latency | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (streaming) | Minimal | Single user | Low | Low | Single user per GPU |
| 4-8 | Moderate | 4-8× larger | Medium | Medium | Optimal for responsiveness |
| 16-32 | High | 16-32× larger | High | High | Multi-user, batch efficiency |
| 64+ | Very High | 64+× larger | Limited | Very High | May OOM or degrade |

**Optimal Target:** **10-20 concurrent requests per GPU** for best throughput/latency balance.

**Formula for max concurrent requests:**
```
Max_Batch_Size = (Available_VRAM - Model_Weights_Size) / (KV_Cache_Per_Request × Bytes_Per_Token)
```

---

### C. Streaming Output

**Token Streaming** sends tokens to the user as they're generated (instead of waiting for full response).

**Effect on GPU:**
- ✅ Reduces **perceived latency** for user
- ❌ Does NOT reduce GPU compute time
- ✅ Improves UX for long responses
- ⚠️ TTFT (Time to First Token) becomes critical

**Best Practice:** Use streaming + optimize TTFT by:
- Reducing batch size on scale-out
- Using prompt caching for repeated system prompts
- Selecting lower-latency instance types (P5 vs P4d)

---

## 5. GPU Selection Matrix: Decision Guide

### Quick Decision Tree

```
START: What's your primary constraint?

├─ COST (budget-sensitive)
│  ├─ Model ≤ 13B → G5.xlarge (A10G)
│  └─ Model 70B → P4d.24xlarge (A100) + auto-scale
│
├─ THROUGHPUT (need to handle many requests)
│  ├─ Model 8B, high volume → P4d or P5
│  └─ Model 70B, very high volume → P5.48xlarge
│
├─ LATENCY (sub-200ms TTFT required)
│  └─ → P5.48xlarge (H100 GPUs)
│
└─ FLEXIBILITY (mix of workloads)
   └─ → Hybrid fleet: G5 + P4d + auto-scaling
```

### Detailed Recommendation Matrix

| Scenario | Recommended Instance | Reasoning | Trade-offs |
|----------|---------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Cost-optimized 7-13B** | G5.xlarge (A10G-24GB) | Best $/GPU | Limited to one model |
| **Throughput 7-13B** | P4d.24xlarge (8× A100) | ~10× throughput over G5 | Higher base cost |
| **Cost-optimized 70B** | P4d.24xlarge (8× A100) | Best $/token for large models | Baseline cost high |
| **Latency-critical (any)** | P5.48xlarge (8× H100) | Fastest GPUs | Premium pricing |
| **Multi-model serving** | Mix: G5 + P4d + auto-scaling | Serve different models on right hardware | Operational complexity |
| **Long context (32K+)** | P5.48xlarge or multi-node P4d | Extra VRAM needed | Significant cost increase |

---

## 6. Optimization Strategies

### Quick Wins (Ranked by Impact)

| Technique | Complexity | Memory Savings | Speed Impact | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|---|---|---|
| **Quantization (INT4)** | Low | 75% | +5-10% faster | No quality loss for inference |
| **Tensor Parallelism** | Medium | Enables 70B+ | Slight overhead | Distributes across GPUs |
| **KV Cache Quantization** | Medium | 50-75% | Negligible | Minimal accuracy impact |
| **Prompt Caching** | Medium | 20-30% | +20-30% latency | For repeated prompts/RAG |
| **vLLM/LMI Optimization** | High | ~5% | +2-3× throughput | Best for multi-user |

### Detailed Strategies

#### 1. **Quantization** (Model Weight Precision)

**How it works:**
```
Original (FP16, 2 bytes/param):  7B params × 2 = 14 GB
INT8 (1 byte/param):             7B params × 1 = 7 GB (50% savings)
INT4 (0.5 bytes/param):          7B params × 0.5 = 3.5 GB (75% savings)
```

**Tools:**
- GPTQ, AWQ (weights only)
- bitsandbytes (INT8)
- vLLM (INT4 with per-channel scaling)

**Trade-off:** <1-2% accuracy loss for most models.

---

#### 2. **Tensor Parallelism**

Distributes model across multiple GPUs on same instance.

**Use When:**
- Model weights exceed single GPU memory (70B on A100-40GB)
- P4d.24xlarge or P5.48xlarge available

**Example:**
```
70B model (140GB FP16) on P4d.24xlarge (8× A100-80GB, 640GB total):
  → Tensor parallelism across all 8 GPUs
  → Each GPU stores 17.5GB model weights
  → Overhead: 10-15% communication cost between GPUs
```

---

#### 3. **KV Cache Quantization**

Store KV cache at lower precision (INT8/INT4) without quantizing model weights.

**Example:**
```
70B model, 4K context, batch 16:
  Model (FP16): 140 GB
  KV Cache (FP16): 70 GB
  Total: 210 GB
  
  With INT4 KV cache:
  Model (FP16): 140 GB
  KV Cache (INT4): 17.5 GB
  Total: 157.5 GB → 25% savings
```

**Impact:** Negligible accuracy loss, significant memory saved.

---

#### 4. **Prompt Caching**

Reuse KV cache for repeated system prompts (e.g., RAG context).

**Example:**
```
System prompt: "You are a helpful assistant..." (500 tokens)
  Without caching: Each request computes 500-token context
  With caching: Reuse 500-token KV cache across requests
  
  Benefit: 20-30% latency reduction for RAG workloads
```

**Tools:** vLLM with `--enable-prefix-caching`

---

#### 5. **vLLM / LMI Optimizations**

Modern inference frameworks with:
- Continuous batching (paged attention)
- Memory paging (reduce fragmentation)
- Speculative decoding

**Throughput Improvement:** 2-3× vs baseline inference.

---

## 7. Auto-Scaling Policies

### Metrics to Monitor

| Metric | Threshold (Scale Up) | Why Monitor | Calculation |
|--------|-----|---|---|
| **RPS** | > 80% of instance max | Direct throughput capacity | Requests processed per second |
| **KV Cache Usage** | > 70% of VRAM | GPU saturation indicator | Monitor via vLLM/LMI stats |
| **Request Queue** | > 5-10 pending | Growing backlog = latency increase | Length of waiting requests |
| **TTFT (Time to First Token)** | > SLA threshold (e.g., 500ms) | User-facing latency | Latency from request to first token |

### Scaling Strategy

**Reactive Scaling (Load-Based):**
```
IF RPS > Threshold
  THEN scale_up_by(1 instance)

IF Queue_Length > Threshold for 5 minutes
  THEN scale_up_by(1 instance)

IF RPS < Threshold AND Average_Utilization < 30% for 10 minutes
  THEN scale_down_by(1 instance)
```

**Scheduled Scaling (Predictable Patterns):**
```
Business hours (9am-6pm): 5 instances
Off-hours (6pm-9am): 2 instances
Weekends: 1 instance
```

### AWS Auto-Scaling Options

| Service | Auto-Scaling Method | Best For |
|---------|---|---|
| **Amazon Bedrock** | Fully managed (no config needed) | Simplicity |
| **SageMaker Endpoints** | Target-based (RPS, custom metrics) | Flexibility |
| **SageMaker HyperPod + EKS** | KEDA / Karpenter | Fine-grained control |
| **ECS Managed Instances** | Task-level scaling | Cost-optimized batch |

---

## 8. Worked Examples: From Requirements to Sizing

### Example 1: Customer Support Chatbot (Small Model)

**Requirements:**
- Model: Llama 2 7B
- Expected users: 100 concurrent
- Avg response length: 150 tokens
- Acceptable latency: < 2 seconds TTFT

**Sizing Calculation:**

```
Step 1: Model Memory
  7B FP16 = 14 GB (model weights)
  KV Cache (4K context, batch ~30) ≈ 7 GB
  Runtime overhead ≈ 2 GB
  Total ≈ 23 GB per GPU
  
Step 2: Single GPU Fit?
  A10G (24GB) ✓ Fits with minimal margin
  
Step 3: Throughput Needed
  RPS = 100 users × 10 req/hr / 3600 = 0.28 requests/sec
  But for concurrency: assume 10 concurrent requests
  Each request needs ~2 seconds, so 10 concurrent = ~5 RPS
  
  Tokens/sec = 5 RPS × 150 tokens = 750 tokens/sec
  
Step 4: Instance Selection
  G5.xlarge: 350 tok/sec → need 750/350 = 2.1 → 3 instances
  Cost: 3 × $0.94/hr = $2.82/hr
  
Step 5: Auto-Scaling
  Min: 1 instance
  Max: 5 instances
  Scale-up trigger: RPS > 280 tok/sec (80% of 350)
  Scale-down trigger: RPS < 70 tok/sec for 10 min
```

**Final Recommendation:**
- **Instance Type:** G5.xlarge (A10G)
- **Instance Count:** 2-3 (with auto-scaling 1-5)
- **Monthly Cost:** ~$2,000-3,000

---

### Example 2: Enterprise RAG System (Large Model)

**Requirements:**
- Model: Llama 2 70B
- Expected QPS: 10 requests/sec (peak)
- Context: 8K (RAG documents)
- Avg response: 300 tokens
- Acceptable latency: < 1 second TTFT (strict)

**Sizing Calculation:**

```
Step 1: Model Memory
  70B FP16 = 140 GB (weights)
  KV Cache (8K context, batch ~20) ≈ 140 GB
  Total ≈ 280 GB (needs tensor parallelism)
  
Step 2: Instance Selection
  Single A100 (80GB) ✗ Insufficient
  P4d.24xlarge (8× A100-80GB, 640GB) ✓ Good fit
  P5.48xlarge (8× H100-80GB, 640GB) ✓ Lower latency
  
Step 3: Throughput Needed
  Tokens/sec = 10 RPS × 300 tokens = 3,000 tokens/sec
  
Step 4: Instance Count
  P4d throughput (70B): ~400 tok/sec
  Instances needed: 3000 / 400 = 7.5 → 8 instances
  
  P5 throughput (70B): ~6500 tok/sec
  Instances needed: 3000 / 6500 = 0.46 → 1 instance ✓
  
Step 5: Cost Comparison
  P4d: 8 × $12.48/hr = $99.84/hr = ~$73K/month
  P5: 1 × $98/hr = $98/hr = ~$71K/month
  
  → P5 better for latency AND cost in this case!
```

**Final Recommendation:**
- **Instance Type:** P5.48xlarge (best latency + cost for high throughput)
- **Instance Count:** 1-2 (with auto-scaling up to 5)
- **Monthly Cost:** ~$70K-140K (depends on load)

---

## 9. Optimization Opportunities

### For Cost:
1. **Use quantization** (INT4) → 75% memory savings
2. **Use G5 for small models** → Best $/GPU
3. **Over-provision slightly & use auto-scale** → Avoid constant max capacity
4. **Use Reserved Instances** (1-3 year) → 30-50% savings

### For Latency:
1. **Use P5.48xlarge** (H100 GPUs) → Lowest latency
2. **Enable prompt caching** → 20-30% faster for RAG
3. **Optimize batch size** → Small batches = lower latency (but worse throughput)
4. **Use streaming responses** → Perceived latency improvement

### For Throughput:
1. **Use tensor parallelism** → Enables large models
2. **Enable vLLM optimizations** → 2-3× improvement
3. **Use continuous batching** → Better GPU utilization
4. **Consider multi-node inference** → Horizontal scale-out

---

## 10. Decision Checklist

Use this checklist before selecting GPU instance:

- [ ] **Model size confirmed** (7B, 13B, 70B, custom?)
- [ ] **Precision decided** (FP16, INT8, INT4?)
- [ ] **Context window defined** (4K, 8K, 32K?)
- [ ] **Expected throughput estimated** (tokens/sec or RPS?)
- [ ] **Latency SLA defined** (TTFT requirement in ms?)
- [ ] **Concurrency estimated** (expected batch size?)
- [ ] **Memory required calculated** (weights + KV cache + overhead?)
- [ ] **Single GPU sufficient?** (or need tensor parallelism?)
- [ ] **Instance type benchmarked** (or use AWS reference?)
- [ ] **Auto-scaling metrics identified** (RPS, queue length, TTFT?)
- [ ] **Cost optimization checked** (quantization? Reserved Instances?)
- [ ] **Fallback plan** (what if scale-out fails due to capacity?)

---

## 11. References & Tools

### AWS Documentation
- [Right-sizing and Auto-scaling - AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/gen-ai-inference-architecture-and-best-practices-on-aws/right-sizing-and-auto-scaling.html)
- [SageMaker LMI (Large Model Inference) Containers](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/optimizing-llm-inference-on-amazon-sagemaker-ai-with-bentomls-llm-optimizer/)

### Benchmarking Tools
- [LLM Stats - Hardware Requirements Calculator](https://llm-stats.com)
- [vLLM Benchmark Suite](https://github.com/vLLM-project/vLLM)
- [Artificial Analysis - GPU Benchmark Comparison](https://artificialanalysis.ai)

### KV Cache Estimation
- [Hugging Face Transformers Calculator](https://huggingface.co)
- [lmcache.ai - KV Cache Size Estimator](https://lmcache.ai)

### Optimization Frameworks
- **vLLM** - High-throughput inference with paged attention
- **LMI (AWS)** - SageMaker LMI containers with vLLM
- **TensorRT-LLM** - NVIDIA optimized inference
- **GPTQ/AWQ** - Model quantization

---

## Final Summary

| Aspect | Key Formula / Rule | Notes |
|--------|-------------------|-------|
| **Memory Sizing** | Weights + KV Cache + 10% overhead | Use AWS reference table as starting point |
| **Throughput** | (Target tok/sec) / (per-instance tok/sec) | Benchmark with actual model on target instance |
| **Latency** | Depends on model size, precision, batch size | Use P5 for <200ms TTFT requirement |
| **Cost Optimization** | Quantization (INT4) + G5 for small models | Can reduce cost 50-75% with <2% accuracy loss |
| **Auto-Scaling** | Monitor RPS, KV cache usage, queue length | Scale on any metric exceeding threshold |

---

**Last Updated:** 2026-06-22 | **Bo (Warot)** | AWS Thailand

# LLM GPU Sizing Guide: Parameters, Throughput & I/O

**Version**: 2026-06-22 | **Based on**: AWS Prescriptive Guidance + Industry Benchmarks

---

## 1. GPU Memory Requirements Formula

### A. Model Weights Memory (Dominant Cost)

**Formula:**
```
Model Weights (GB) = Parameters (Billions) × Bytes per Parameter
```

**Precision Levels & Their Impact:**

| Precision | Bits | Bytes/Param | 7B Model | 13B Model | 70B Model |
|-----------|------|------------|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **FP32** (Full) | 32 | 4 | 28 GB | 52 GB | 280 GB |
| **FP16** (Half) | 16 | 2 | 14 GB | 26 GB | 140 GB ⭐ |
| **INT8** (Quantized) | 8 | 1 | 7 GB | 13 GB | 70 GB |
| **INT4** (Compressed) | 4 | 0.5 | 3.5 GB | 6.5 GB | 35 GB |

**Key Insight:** Quantization from FP16 → INT4 saves 75% memory with <1-2% accuracy loss.

---

### B. KV Cache (Key-Value Cache) — Attention Memory

The KV cache stores processed input/output tokens across all transformer attention layers.

**Formula (Approximate):**
```
KV Cache (GB) ≈ 2 × Context_Length × Batch_Size × Num_Heads × Bytes_Per_Token
```

**Rule of Thumb:**
- **Baseline**: KV cache ≈ **50% of model weights** for typical workloads
- **Long context** (8K-32K tokens): Can exceed model weights
- **High concurrency** (batch size > 32): KV cache dominates memory

**Scaling Factors:**

| Factor | Impact | Example |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **Context Length** | Linear | 4K → 8K = 2× KV cache |
| **Batch Size** | Linear | Batch 4 → 16 = 4× KV cache |
| **Attention Heads** | Linear | 32 → 128 heads = 4× KV cache |
| **Precision** | Linear | FP16 → INT8 = 50% reduction |

---

### C. Total GPU Memory Breakdown

**Typical Distribution for Inference:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Total GPU Memory Budget                │
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Model Weights         70-75% ████████  │
│  KV Cache              15-20% ██        │
│  Runtime Overhead       5-10% █         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Runtime Overhead Includes:**
- CUDA context initialization
- Activation tensors during forward pass
- Framework buffers (PyTorch, vLLM, etc.)

**Total Formula:**
```
Total Memory Needed = Model Weights × (1 + KV_Cache_Factor + Overhead_Factor)
                    ≈ Model Weights × 1.6  (for typical small batch inference)
```

---

## 2. AWS Reference: Minimum GPU Memory by Model Size

**Source:** [AWS Prescriptive Guidance - Right-sizing an Inference System](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/gen-ai-inference-architecture-and-best-practices-on-aws/right-sizing-and-auto-scaling.html)

| Model Size | FP16 (2-byte) | INT8 (1-byte) | INT4 (0.5-byte) | Notes |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|--------------------|-------|
| **7B** | 14 GB | 7 GB | 3.5 GB | Fits A10G with headroom |
| **13B** | 26 GB | 13 GB | 6.5 GB | Fits A100-40GB |
| **70B** | 140 GB | 70 GB | 35 GB | Requires tensor parallelism ↓ |

**Add 20-50% margin** for KV cache + runtime overhead.

**Practical Examples:**
- **7B FP16 (14 GB)** → Use A100-40GB or A10G-24GB with INT4
- **13B FP16 (26 GB)** → Use A100-40GB or A100-80GB
- **70B FP16 (140 GB)** → Use P4d (8× A100-80GB) or P5 (8× H100-80GB) with tensor parallelism

---

## 3. Token Throughput & Compute Requirements

### A. Key Performance Metrics

| Metric | Definition | Why It Matters |
|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **RPS** (Requests/Sec) | Number of inference requests per second | Measures throughput capacity |
| **Tokens/Sec** | Total output tokens generated per second | Direct cost driver |
| **TTFT** (Time to First Token) | Latency from request to first output token | User-facing latency |
| **Tokens Generated** | Avg output tokens per request | Affects total cost & latency |

**Relationships:**
```
Tokens/Sec = RPS × Avg_Output_Tokens_Per_Request
```

---

### B. EC2 GPU Instance Throughput Benchmarks (2026)

**8B Model Inference:**

| Instance Type | GPU | GPU Memory | Throughput | Cost per 1M tokens | Use Case |
|---------------|-----|-----------|------------|-------------------|----------|
| **G5.xlarge** | A10G | 24 GB | ~350 tok/s | $1.50 | Cost-optimized |
| **P4d.24xlarge** | 8× A100-80GB | 640 GB | ~3,800 tok/s | $0.85 | High throughput |
| **P5.48xlarge** | 8× H100-80GB | 640 GB | ~7,000 tok/s | $2.02 | Latency-critical |

**70B Model Inference (with tensor parallelism):**

| Instance Type | Throughput | TTFT | Cost/1M tokens | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P4d.24xlarge** | ~400 tok/s | ~50-80ms | $0.90 | Cost-optimized for 70B |
| **P5.48xlarge** | ~4,500-6,500 tok/s | ~20-30ms | $2.50 | High throughput |

**Key Insight:**
- **G5 is best for cost** (smaller models ≤ 13B)
- **P4d is best for cost/performance** (70B models)
- **P5 is best for latency** (latency-critical apps)

---

### C. Estimating Required Instances

**Formula:**
```
Required Instances = Target_Throughput_Tokens_Per_Sec / Per_Instance_Throughput
```

**Example 1: Small model, moderate throughput**
```
Use case: 8B model, need 1,000 tokens/sec
  → G5.xlarge throughput: 350 tok/s
  → Instances needed: 1000 / 350 = 2.9 → 3 instances
  → Cost: 3 × $0.94/hr = $2.82/hr
```

**Example 2: Large model, high throughput**
```
Use case: 70B model, need 2,000 tokens/sec
  → P4d.24xlarge throughput: 400 tok/s
  → Instances needed: 2000 / 400 = 5 instances
  → Cost: 5 × $12.48/hr = $62.40/hr (or $2.02/M tokens)
```

**Example 3: Large model, very high throughput**
```
Use case: 70B model, need 5,000 tokens/sec
  → P4d.24xlarge: need 13 instances = $162/hr
  → P5.48xlarge: need 1 instance = $98/hr ✓ (better value for high throughput)
```

---

## 4. Input/Output (I/O) Considerations

### A. Context Window Impact

**How Context Length Affects GPU Requirements:**

| Context Length | KV Cache Factor | Example (70B model) |
|---|---|---|
| 4K (short) | ~50% of weights | 140GB weights + 70GB KV = 210GB |
| 8K (medium) | ~100% of weights | 140GB weights + 140GB KV = 280GB |
| 32K (long) | ~400% of weights | 140GB weights + 560GB KV = 700GB |

**Impact on Throughput:**
- Longer context = more attention computations
- **Throughput degrades** ~linearly with context length
- 4K context might get 400 tok/s, but 32K context might drop to 100 tok/s

**Implication:** Long context (32K+) workloads require larger instances or more inference nodes.

---

### B. Batch Size & Concurrency

**Batch Size**: Number of concurrent requests processed on the same GPU at once.

| Batch Size | Memory Impact | KV Cache Growth | Throughput | Latency | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (streaming) | Minimal | Single user | Low | Low | Single user per GPU |
| 4-8 | Moderate | 4-8× larger | Medium | Medium | Optimal for responsiveness |
| 16-32 | High | 16-32× larger | High | High | Multi-user, batch efficiency |
| 64+ | Very High | 64+× larger | Limited | Very High | May OOM or degrade |

**Optimal Target:** **10-20 concurrent requests per GPU** for best throughput/latency balance.

**Formula for max concurrent requests:**
```
Max_Batch_Size = (Available_VRAM - Model_Weights_Size) / (KV_Cache_Per_Request × Bytes_Per_Token)
```

---

### C. Streaming Output

**Token Streaming** sends tokens to the user as they're generated (instead of waiting for full response).

**Effect on GPU:**
- ✅ Reduces **perceived latency** for user
- ❌ Does NOT reduce GPU compute time
- ✅ Improves UX for long responses
- ⚠️ TTFT (Time to First Token) becomes critical

**Best Practice:** Use streaming + optimize TTFT by:
- Reducing batch size on scale-out
- Using prompt caching for repeated system prompts
- Selecting lower-latency instance types (P5 vs P4d)

---

## 5. GPU Selection Matrix: Decision Guide

### Quick Decision Tree

```
START: What's your primary constraint?

├─ COST (budget-sensitive)
│  ├─ Model ≤ 13B → G5.xlarge (A10G)
│  └─ Model 70B → P4d.24xlarge (A100) + auto-scale
│
├─ THROUGHPUT (need to handle many requests)
│  ├─ Model 8B, high volume → P4d or P5
│  └─ Model 70B, very high volume → P5.48xlarge
│
├─ LATENCY (sub-200ms TTFT required)
│  └─ → P5.48xlarge (H100 GPUs)
│
└─ FLEXIBILITY (mix of workloads)
   └─ → Hybrid fleet: G5 + P4d + auto-scaling
```

### Detailed Recommendation Matrix

| Scenario | Recommended Instance | Reasoning | Trade-offs |
|----------|---------------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Cost-optimized 7-13B** | G5.xlarge (A10G-24GB) | Best $/GPU | Limited to one model |
| **Throughput 7-13B** | P4d.24xlarge (8× A100) | ~10× throughput over G5 | Higher base cost |
| **Cost-optimized 70B** | P4d.24xlarge (8× A100) | Best $/token for large models | Baseline cost high |
| **Latency-critical (any)** | P5.48xlarge (8× H100) | Fastest GPUs | Premium pricing |
| **Multi-model serving** | Mix: G5 + P4d + auto-scaling | Serve different models on right hardware | Operational complexity |
| **Long context (32K+)** | P5.48xlarge or multi-node P4d | Extra VRAM needed | Significant cost increase |

---

## 6. Optimization Strategies

### Quick Wins (Ranked by Impact)

| Technique | Complexity | Memory Savings | Speed Impact | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|---|---|---|
| **Quantization (INT4)** | Low | 75% | +5-10% faster | No quality loss for inference |
| **Tensor Parallelism** | Medium | Enables 70B+ | Slight overhead | Distributes across GPUs |
| **KV Cache Quantization** | Medium | 50-75% | Negligible | Minimal accuracy impact |
| **Prompt Caching** | Medium | 20-30% | +20-30% latency | For repeated prompts/RAG |
| **vLLM/LMI Optimization** | High | ~5% | +2-3× throughput | Best for multi-user |

### Detailed Strategies

#### 1. **Quantization** (Model Weight Precision)

**How it works:**
```
Original (FP16, 2 bytes/param):  7B params × 2 = 14 GB
INT8 (1 byte/param):             7B params × 1 = 7 GB (50% savings)
INT4 (0.5 bytes/param):          7B params × 0.5 = 3.5 GB (75% savings)
```

**Tools:**
- GPTQ, AWQ (weights only)
- bitsandbytes (INT8)
- vLLM (INT4 with per-channel scaling)

**Trade-off:** <1-2% accuracy loss for most models.

---

#### 2. **Tensor Parallelism**

Distributes model across multiple GPUs on same instance.

**Use When:**
- Model weights exceed single GPU memory (70B on A100-40GB)
- P4d.24xlarge or P5.48xlarge available

**Example:**
```
70B model (140GB FP16) on P4d.24xlarge (8× A100-80GB, 640GB total):
  → Tensor parallelism across all 8 GPUs
  → Each GPU stores 17.5GB model weights
  → Overhead: 10-15% communication cost between GPUs
```

---

#### 3. **KV Cache Quantization**

Store KV cache at lower precision (INT8/INT4) without quantizing model weights.

**Example:**
```
70B model, 4K context, batch 16:
  Model (FP16): 140 GB
  KV Cache (FP16): 70 GB
  Total: 210 GB
  
  With INT4 KV cache:
  Model (FP16): 140 GB
  KV Cache (INT4): 17.5 GB
  Total: 157.5 GB → 25% savings
```

**Impact:** Negligible accuracy loss, significant memory saved.

---

#### 4. **Prompt Caching**

Reuse KV cache for repeated system prompts (e.g., RAG context).

**Example:**
```
System prompt: "You are a helpful assistant..." (500 tokens)
  Without caching: Each request computes 500-token context
  With caching: Reuse 500-token KV cache across requests
  
  Benefit: 20-30% latency reduction for RAG workloads
```

**Tools:** vLLM with `--enable-prefix-caching`

---

#### 5. **vLLM / LMI Optimizations**

Modern inference frameworks with:
- Continuous batching (paged attention)
- Memory paging (reduce fragmentation)
- Speculative decoding

**Throughput Improvement:** 2-3× vs baseline inference.

---

## 7. Auto-Scaling Policies

### Metrics to Monitor

| Metric | Threshold (Scale Up) | Why Monitor | Calculation |
|--------|-----|---|---|
| **RPS** | > 80% of instance max | Direct throughput capacity | Requests processed per second |
| **KV Cache Usage** | > 70% of VRAM | GPU saturation indicator | Monitor via vLLM/LMI stats |
| **Request Queue** | > 5-10 pending | Growing backlog = latency increase | Length of waiting requests |
| **TTFT (Time to First Token)** | > SLA threshold (e.g., 500ms) | User-facing latency | Latency from request to first token |

### Scaling Strategy

**Reactive Scaling (Load-Based):**
```
IF RPS > Threshold
  THEN scale_up_by(1 instance)

IF Queue_Length > Threshold for 5 minutes
  THEN scale_up_by(1 instance)

IF RPS < Threshold AND Average_Utilization < 30% for 10 minutes
  THEN scale_down_by(1 instance)
```

**Scheduled Scaling (Predictable Patterns):**
```
Business hours (9am-6pm): 5 instances
Off-hours (6pm-9am): 2 instances
Weekends: 1 instance
```

### AWS Auto-Scaling Options

| Service | Auto-Scaling Method | Best For |
|---------|---|---|
| **Amazon Bedrock** | Fully managed (no config needed) | Simplicity |
| **SageMaker Endpoints** | Target-based (RPS, custom metrics) | Flexibility |
| **SageMaker HyperPod + EKS** | KEDA / Karpenter | Fine-grained control |
| **ECS Managed Instances** | Task-level scaling | Cost-optimized batch |

---

## 8. Worked Examples: From Requirements to Sizing

### Example 1: Customer Support Chatbot (Small Model)

**Requirements:**
- Model: Llama 2 7B
- Expected users: 100 concurrent
- Avg response length: 150 tokens
- Acceptable latency: < 2 seconds TTFT

**Sizing Calculation:**

```
Step 1: Model Memory
  7B FP16 = 14 GB (model weights)
  KV Cache (4K context, batch ~30) ≈ 7 GB
  Runtime overhead ≈ 2 GB
  Total ≈ 23 GB per GPU
  
Step 2: Single GPU Fit?
  A10G (24GB) ✓ Fits with minimal margin
  
Step 3: Throughput Needed
  RPS = 100 users × 10 req/hr / 3600 = 0.28 requests/sec
  But for concurrency: assume 10 concurrent requests
  Each request needs ~2 seconds, so 10 concurrent = ~5 RPS
  
  Tokens/sec = 5 RPS × 150 tokens = 750 tokens/sec
  
Step 4: Instance Selection
  G5.xlarge: 350 tok/sec → need 750/350 = 2.1 → 3 instances
  Cost: 3 × $0.94/hr = $2.82/hr
  
Step 5: Auto-Scaling
  Min: 1 instance
  Max: 5 instances
  Scale-up trigger: RPS > 280 tok/sec (80% of 350)
  Scale-down trigger: RPS < 70 tok/sec for 10 min
```

**Final Recommendation:**
- **Instance Type:** G5.xlarge (A10G)
- **Instance Count:** 2-3 (with auto-scaling 1-5)
- **Monthly Cost:** ~$2,000-3,000

---

### Example 2: Enterprise RAG System (Large Model)

**Requirements:**
- Model: Llama 2 70B
- Expected QPS: 10 requests/sec (peak)
- Context: 8K (RAG documents)
- Avg response: 300 tokens
- Acceptable latency: < 1 second TTFT (strict)

**Sizing Calculation:**

```
Step 1: Model Memory
  70B FP16 = 140 GB (weights)
  KV Cache (8K context, batch ~20) ≈ 140 GB
  Total ≈ 280 GB (needs tensor parallelism)
  
Step 2: Instance Selection
  Single A100 (80GB) ✗ Insufficient
  P4d.24xlarge (8× A100-80GB, 640GB) ✓ Good fit
  P5.48xlarge (8× H100-80GB, 640GB) ✓ Lower latency
  
Step 3: Throughput Needed
  Tokens/sec = 10 RPS × 300 tokens = 3,000 tokens/sec
  
Step 4: Instance Count
  P4d throughput (70B): ~400 tok/sec
  Instances needed: 3000 / 400 = 7.5 → 8 instances
  
  P5 throughput (70B): ~6500 tok/sec
  Instances needed: 3000 / 6500 = 0.46 → 1 instance ✓
  
Step 5: Cost Comparison
  P4d: 8 × $12.48/hr = $99.84/hr = ~$73K/month
  P5: 1 × $98/hr = $98/hr = ~$71K/month
  
  → P5 better for latency AND cost in this case!
```

**Final Recommendation:**
- **Instance Type:** P5.48xlarge (best latency + cost for high throughput)
- **Instance Count:** 1-2 (with auto-scaling up to 5)
- **Monthly Cost:** ~$70K-140K (depends on load)

---

## 9. Optimization Opportunities

### For Cost:
1. **Use quantization** (INT4) → 75% memory savings
2. **Use G5 for small models** → Best $/GPU
3. **Over-provision slightly & use auto-scale** → Avoid constant max capacity
4. **Use Reserved Instances** (1-3 year) → 30-50% savings

### For Latency:
1. **Use P5.48xlarge** (H100 GPUs) → Lowest latency
2. **Enable prompt caching** → 20-30% faster for RAG
3. **Optimize batch size** → Small batches = lower latency (but worse throughput)
4. **Use streaming responses** → Perceived latency improvement

### For Throughput:
1. **Use tensor parallelism** → Enables large models
2. **Enable vLLM optimizations** → 2-3× improvement
3. **Use continuous batching** → Better GPU utilization
4. **Consider multi-node inference** → Horizontal scale-out

---

## 10. Decision Checklist

Use this checklist before selecting GPU instance:

- [ ] **Model size confirmed** (7B, 13B, 70B, custom?)
- [ ] **Precision decided** (FP16, INT8, INT4?)
- [ ] **Context window defined** (4K, 8K, 32K?)
- [ ] **Expected throughput estimated** (tokens/sec or RPS?)
- [ ] **Latency SLA defined** (TTFT requirement in ms?)
- [ ] **Concurrency estimated** (expected batch size?)
- [ ] **Memory required calculated** (weights + KV cache + overhead?)
- [ ] **Single GPU sufficient?** (or need tensor parallelism?)
- [ ] **Instance type benchmarked** (or use AWS reference?)
- [ ] **Auto-scaling metrics identified** (RPS, queue length, TTFT?)
- [ ] **Cost optimization checked** (quantization? Reserved Instances?)
- [ ] **Fallback plan** (what if scale-out fails due to capacity?)

---

## 11. References & Tools

### AWS Documentation
- [Right-sizing and Auto-scaling - AWS Prescriptive Guidance](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance/latest/gen-ai-inference-architecture-and-best-practices-on-aws/right-sizing-and-auto-scaling.html)
- [SageMaker LMI (Large Model Inference) Containers](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/optimizing-llm-inference-on-amazon-sagemaker-ai-with-bentomls-llm-optimizer/)

### Benchmarking Tools
- [LLM Stats - Hardware Requirements Calculator](https://llm-stats.com)
- [vLLM Benchmark Suite](https://github.com/vLLM-project/vLLM)
- [Artificial Analysis - GPU Benchmark Comparison](https://artificialanalysis.ai)

### KV Cache Estimation
- [Hugging Face Transformers Calculator](https://huggingface.co)

### Optimization Frameworks
- **vLLM** - High-throughput inference with paged attention
- **LMI (AWS)** - SageMaker LMI containers with vLLM
- **TensorRT-LLM** - NVIDIA optimized inference
- **GPTQ/AWQ** - Model quantization

---

## Final Summary

| Aspect | Key Formula / Rule | Notes |
|--------|-------------------|-------|
| **Memory Sizing** | Weights + KV Cache + 10% overhead | Use AWS reference table as starting point |
| **Throughput** | (Target tok/sec) / (per-instance tok/sec) | Benchmark with actual model on target instance |

# Frontend Design Steering — Theme Selection Wizard

## Purpose

This steering governs ALL frontend UI generation. Before writing any frontend code (HTML, CSS, React, Vue, etc.), you MUST present the Theme Selection Wizard below and wait for the user to choose.

---

## 🎨 Theme Selection Wizard

**Before generating ANY frontend UI, present this wizard:**

> **Which visual theme should I use for this frontend?**
>
> Pick a number (1–20):
>
> | # | Theme | Vibe |
> |---|-------|------|
> | 1 | **Neubrutalism** | Bold colors, thick black borders, hard offset shadows |
> | 2 | **Kinetic Typography** | Giant text IS the design — words overlap, rotate, dominate |
> | 3 | **Sketchy Wireframe** | Hand-drawn wobbly lines, gray/white, napkin-sketch feel |
> | 4 | **Aurora UI** | Ethereal gradient mesh, northern-lights glow, glassmorphism |
> | 5 | **Bento Grid** | Japanese bento-box modular tiles, asymmetric layout |
> | 6 | **Anti-Design** | Punk rock chaos — clashing colors, broken grids |
> | 7 | **Dark Precision SaaS** | Linear/Vercel vibe — near-black, razor-sharp, restrained |
> | 8 | **Dark Maximalism** | Dark mode meets baroque — gold accents, ornate textures |
> | 9 | **Liquid Motion** | Fluid morphing blobs, organic curves, living UI |
> | 10 | **Monochrome Luxe** | Single-color palette, editorial typography, pure elegance |
> | 11 | **Cyberpunk HUD** | Neon glitch, scan lines, sci-fi tactical interface |
> | 12 | **Organic Minimalism** | Earth tones, nature curves, ceramics studio calm |
> | 13 | **Collage Punk** | Zine aesthetic — torn textures, stamps, scissors-and-glue |
> | 14 | **Swiss Clean** | Strict grid, Helvetica worldview, form follows function |
> | 15 | **High-Contrast Duotone** | Two colors max, bold readability, accessibility-first |
> | 16 | **Grain & Texture Analog** | Film grain, vintage tones, analog warmth |
> | 17 | **Blueprint Wireframe** | Technical drawing grid, annotation labels, architecture UI |
> | 18 | **Editorial Serif** | Vogue magazine meets dashboard — serif, pull-quotes |
> | 19 | **MySpace / Geocities** | 1999 homepage chaos — neon links, tiled backgrounds |
> | 20 | **shadcn Default** | Clean zinc grays, subtle borders, polished dev defaults |

**Wait for the user's selection before proceeding.**

---

## Theme Definitions

Once the user selects a theme, apply the corresponding style rules below for ALL frontend code generation in the session.

### Theme 1 — Neubrutalism
- **Accent**: `#FF5C00`
- **Tags**: raw, bold-shadows, exposed
- **Direction**: Bold saturated colors, thick black borders, hard offset drop shadows, zero rounded corners, raw structural energy with playful confidence. Everything boxed, everything unapologetic. Cards feel stamped onto the page.

### Theme 2 — Kinetic Typography
- **Accent**: `#1A1A1A`
- **Tags**: typography, kinetic, oversized
- **Direction**: TEXT IS THE ENTIRE DESIGN. No icons, no illustrations — only letters and numbers. Metrics as GIANT numerals (200px+). Words overlap, stack, intersect. Mix ultra-bold condensed and ultra-thin extended weights. Monochrome with ONE color accent on one word. David Carson's Ray Gun magazine as a dashboard.

### Theme 3 — Sketchy Wireframe
- **Accent**: `#9CA3AF`
- **Tags**: wireframe, sketch, minimal, lo-fi
- **Direction**: Hand-drawn aesthetic — ALL lines squiggly via SVG filters (feTurbulence + feDisplacementMap) or irregular border-radius. Thin wobbly borders in gray. Handwriting fonts ('Architects Daughter', 'Patrick Hand', 'Caveat'). Placeholder-style content blocks, cross-hatching for fills. NO color beyond grays and white.

### Theme 4 — Aurora UI
- **Accent**: `#6366F1`
- **Tags**: mesh-gradient, luminous, modern
- **Direction**: Dark base with ethereal gradient mesh backgrounds, northern-lights color flows using layered radial gradients, glassmorphism cards with backdrop-blur, soft luminous depth. Background feels alive and atmospheric.

### Theme 5 — Bento Grid
- **Accent**: `#0A84FF`
- **Tags**: bento, modular, oversized
- **Direction**: Japanese bento-box modular layout with genuinely asymmetric tiles of varying sizes (2×2, 1×1, 1×2), oversized typography inside tiles, clean compartmentalized sections. The grid itself is the design statement.

### Theme 6 — Anti-Design
- **Accent**: `#FF0040`
- **Tags**: chaotic, punk, rule-breaking
- **Direction**: Intentionally clashing colors, broken grid layouts, wildly mixed typefaces and sizes, rotated elements, overlapping content. Dissonance and tension are the point. Rules exist to be violated with attitude.

### Theme 7 — Dark Precision SaaS
- **Accent**: `#A78BFA`
- **Tags**: dark, precise, saas
- **Direction**: Near-black backgrounds, ultra-subtle borders, muted accent used sparingly, razor-sharp compact typography. Linear/Vercel/Raycast aesthetic. Precision engineering made visible through restrained elegance. Compact, information-dense, nothing wasted.

### Theme 8 — Dark Maximalism
- **Accent**: `#F59E0B`
- **Tags**: dark, rich, maximalist
- **Direction**: Rich dark palette with warm undertones, layered surfaces with visible depth, gold and jewel-tone accents, ornate decorative details, noise/grain textures, dramatic typography mixing serif display and sans-serif body. Baroque ambition meets dark mode.

### Theme 9 — Liquid Motion
- **Accent**: `#8B5CF6`
- **Tags**: fluid, organic, animated
- **Direction**: Fluid morphing blob shapes as backgrounds, organic curves everywhere, pill-shaped buttons, large rounded corners, elastic hover animations, gradient fills that shift on interaction. The UI feels like a living organism — nothing static, everything breathes.

### Theme 10 — Monochrome Luxe
- **Accent**: `#1A1A2E`
- **Tags**: monochrome, editorial, luxe
- **Direction**: Strictly black, white, and grays with one deep accent used sparingly. All visual interest from typography and space. Rich serif display headings, clean sans-serif body. Generous whitespace, editorial column structure, hairline rules. Constraint breeds elegance.

### Theme 11 — Cyberpunk HUD
- **Accent**: `#00FFFF`
- **Tags**: hud, glitch, tactical
- **Direction**: Very dark background, neon accents (cyan, magenta), monospace typography, dense tactical layout, scanline overlays, glitch animations, corner-bracket framing, neon glow on borders and text. Sci-fi dashboard through augmented-reality implants.

### Theme 12 — Organic Minimalism
- **Accent**: `#5B7553`
- **Tags**: earth, organic, nature
- **Direction**: Warm earth tones (olive, clay, sage, stone), generous whitespace, flowing organic composition, large rounded corners, soft warm shadows. Hand-drawn SVG illustrations. Nature-inspired curves replace geometric rigidity. Ceramics studio confidence.

### Theme 13 — Collage Punk
- **Accent**: `#EF4444`
- **Tags**: mixed-media, zine, lo-fi
- **Direction**: Zine aesthetic — mixed typefaces (typewriter, handwritten, bold sans), cards at slight rotations, tape-and-sticker elements, torn paper edges, newsprint textures, stamp-like badges. Lo-fi and tactile, assembled with scissors and glue.

### Theme 14 — Swiss Clean
- **Accent**: `#FF0000`
- **Tags**: grid, helvetica, clean
- **Direction**: Strict mathematical grid, black and white with one red accent, left-aligned everything, clear typographic hierarchy through size and weight alone, zero decorative elements, generous whitespace. The grid IS the decoration.

### Theme 15 — High-Contrast Duotone
- **Accent**: `#FFD600`
- **Tags**: accessible, duotone, stark
- **Direction**: Strictly two base colors (black and white) with one bright accent. No mid-tones. Large legible typography, thick borders, oversized touch targets, bold foreground/background separation. Accessibility as aesthetic statement. Stark, surgical, impossible to misread.

### Theme 16 — Grain & Texture Analog
- **Accent**: `#B45309`
- **Tags**: grain, analog, vintage
- **Direction**: Visible noise/grain overlays on every surface, muted vintage tones (rust, ochre, olive, slate), warm off-white backgrounds like aged paper, slightly desaturated faded colors. Film-stock warmth and tactile depth. Classic serif/sans with typewriter accents.

### Theme 17 — Blueprint Wireframe
- **Accent**: `#3B82F6`
- **Tags**: blueprint, technical, exposed
- **Direction**: Visible graph-paper grid lines as primary structure, monospace annotation labels, measurement lines and dimension callouts, crosshair markers, corner coordinates. The interface shows its own construction like a technical drawing.

### Theme 18 — Editorial Serif
- **Accent**: `#B91C1C`
- **Tags**: serif, editorial, magazine
- **Direction**: Sophisticated serif typography (Playfair Display or DM Serif Display) for ALL headings, dramatic size contrast (72px+ headlines, 14px body), generous whitespace (40-60px+ padding), thin 1px rule separators, pull-quote callouts, asymmetric magazine columns, black-and-cream with deep red accent. Vogue's art director designed your dashboard.

### Theme 19 — MySpace / Geocities
- **Accent**: `#FF00FF`
- **Tags**: retro-web, chaotic, maximalist, nostalgic
- **Direction**: Late-90s personal homepage chaos. Tiled backgrounds, hot pink + electric blue + lime green, Comic Sans mixed with Times New Roman, fake visitor counters, marquee-style scrolling (CSS animation), beveled borders, sparkle decorations, "under construction" energy. Maximum self-expression, zero design system, pure creative joy.

### Theme 20 — shadcn Default
- **Accent**: `#18181B`
- **Tags**: clean, minimal, saas, modern
- **Direction**: shadcn/ui default theme. White backgrounds, zinc-50 (#FAFAFA) surfaces, zinc-200 (#E4E4E7) borders (1px solid). Inter or Geist Sans, 14px base. Small border-radius (6px buttons, 8px cards). Primary button: solid black with white text. Cards with thin border + shadow-sm. Consistent 16px/24px spacing. Zero decorative elements — spacing and consistency IS the design.

---

## Global Design Rules (Apply to ALL Themes)

These rules apply regardless of which theme is selected:

### Production-Grade Code
- Complete, self-contained HTML with ZERO external dependencies (no CDN scripts)
- ALL styles in a single `<style>` block using plain CSS
- CSS custom properties (`--color-*`, `--font-*`, `--radius-*`) for theming
- All icons as inline SVG

### Responsive Design
- Include `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">`
- Mobile-first with CSS media queries to scale up
- At 375px: stack sidebars vertically, single-column grids, proportional typography, no horizontal overflow
- `html` and `body` at `width: 100%` with no horizontal margin/padding
- Sidebar layouts: CSS grid/flex where sidebar + main span 100% viewport

### Typography
- NEVER use generic fonts (Arial, Inter, Roboto, system fonts) unless the theme explicitly specifies them
- Choose distinctive, characterful fonts that match the theme
- Pair display fonts with refined body fonts

### Visual Quality
- Dominant colors with sharp accents — avoid timid, evenly-distributed palettes
- CSS-only animations preferred for HTML output
- Unexpected layouts: asymmetry, overlap, diagonal flow, grid-breaking elements
- Atmosphere and depth over solid color backgrounds

### Anti-Patterns (NEVER Do)
- Generic "AI slop" aesthetics
- Purple gradients on white backgrounds (unless theme-specific)
- Predictable layouts and cookie-cutter components
- Converging on the same fonts across different themes

content.data:
# IAC Security Steering Rules

These rules MUST be followed when generating any Infrastructure as Code templates (CDK, CloudFormation, Terraform, SAM, etc.).

## 1. No Public S3 Buckets

- NEVER create S3 buckets with public access enabled.
- All S3 buckets MUST have `BlockPublicAccess` set to block all public access.
- If content needs to be publicly accessible, use a **private S3 bucket + CloudFront distribution** with an Origin Access Control (OAC) policy.
- Always enable S3 bucket encryption (SSE-S3 or SSE-KMS).

## 2. No Public Databases — Private Subnets Only

- NEVER place any database (RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB VPC endpoints, ElastiCache, DocumentDB, Neptune, Redshift, etc.) in a public subnet.
- All databases MUST be deployed in **private subnets** with no public IP assignment.
- Set `PubliclyAccessible: false` (or equivalent) on all database resources.
- Database security groups MUST only allow ingress from application subnets, never from `0.0.0.0/0`.

## 3. Applications in Private Subnets — Expose via ELB or API Gateway Only

- NEVER deploy application compute (EC2, ECS, EKS, Lambda in VPC, etc.) in public subnets.
- All application workloads MUST run in **private subnets**.
- Public-facing access MUST be provided exclusively through:
  - **Application Load Balancer (ALB)** or **Network Load Balancer (NLB)** in public subnets, OR
  - **API Gateway** (REST or HTTP API).
- Use NAT Gateways in public subnets for outbound internet access from private subnets when needed.
- ALB listener rules MUST include a **host-header condition** matching the application's domain name to prevent direct access via the ALB's IP address. The default listener action MUST return a fixed `403 Forbidden` response to reject any request that does not match a valid host header.

Done! Here's what was created:

## Project Structure

```
/home/ec2-user/llm-sizer/
├── index.html                          # Static LLM GPU Sizer (Aurora UI theme)
├── LLM GPU Sizing Guide.md
└── infrastructure/                     # CDK project (ap-southeast-7)
    ├── bin/infrastructure.ts           # Stack entry point
    └── lib/infrastructure-stack.ts     # S3 + CloudFront stack
```

## Static Site (`index.html`)

- **Aurora UI theme**: dark base with ethereal gradient mesh backgrounds, glassmorphism cards, northern-lights animation
- **Inputs**: Model parameters (B), precision (FP32/FP16/INT8/INT4), context length (4K–32K), batch size
- **Outputs**: Model weights memory, KV cache memory, total GPU memory required
- **Recommendation**: Best-fit AWS GPU instance (G5 / P4d / P5) with throughput & cost info, tensor parallelism indicator

## CDK Infrastructure (iac-security compliant)

- ✅ S3 bucket with `BlockPublicAccess.BLOCK_ALL` + S3-managed encryption
- ✅ CloudFront distribution with **Origin Access Control (OAC)** — no public S3 access
- ✅ HTTPS redirect enforced
- ✅ Auto-deploys `index.html` to S3 on `cdk deploy`
- ✅ Targets `ap-southeast-7`

## Deploy

```bash
cd /home/ec2-user/llm-sizer/infrastructure
npx cdk deploy
```

The CloudFront URL will be output after deployment.
Please check if you are using S3 static web hosting without public access bucket + CloudFront deployment and then create deploy.sh with emojis and color for quick deploy script

- g5.xlarge ($686/mo) through p5.48xlarge ($71,773/mo)

Changes to the Break-Even Calculator:

1. **Slider for I/O ratio** — Drag from 0% to 100% input (with live label showing e.g. "75% input / 25% output"). Much easier than a dropdown.

2. **Recommended instance auto-selected per model** — When you pick a Bedrock model, the Self-Host Instance dropdown automatically selects the best-fit GPU instance (with a brief ⭐ badge). The mapping:
   - ≤13B models → g5.xlarge
   - ~24-30B models → g6e.xlarge (L40S 48GB)
   - 70-80B models → g6e.12xlarge (4× L40S)
   - 100-235B models → p4d.24xlarge (8× A100)
   - 400B+ / MoE models → p5.48xlarge (8× H100)

- Added throughout — GPU card, CPU, calculator, server, memory, lightning, graphs, etc.
- Icons in card headers, labels, table headers, metric boxes, and recommendation items

**Improved Layout:**
- Dedicated header section with large GPU icon
- Card headers with icon + title pattern
- Table wrappers with proper overflow handling and rounded borders
- Better spacing, larger touch targets

- Footer with branding
- Stronger visual hierarchy with uppercase labels, letter-spacing, and weight contrast

#!/bin/bash
set -e

RED='\033[0;31m'
GREEN='\033[0;32m'
YELLOW='\033[1;33m'
BLUE='\033[0;34m'
PURPLE='\033[0;35m'
CYAN='\033[0;36m'
NC='\033[0m'

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"

echo ""
echo -e "${PURPLE}╔══════════════════════════════════════════════╗${NC}"
echo -e "${PURPLE}║${NC}  🚀 ${CYAN}LLM GPU Sizer — Production Deploy${NC}       ${PURPLE}║${NC}"
echo -e "${PURPLE}║${NC}  📍 ${YELLOW}Region: ap-southeast-7${NC}                  ${PURPLE}║${NC}"
echo -e "${PURPLE}╚══════════════════════════════════════════════╝${NC}"
echo ""

# Get the S3 bucket and distribution ID from CloudFormation outputs
get_stack_resources() {
    BUCKET=$(aws cloudformation describe-stack-resources \
        --stack-name LlmGpuSizerStack \
        --region ap-southeast-7 \
        --query "StackResources[?ResourceType=='AWS::S3::Bucket'].PhysicalResourceId" \
        --output text 2>/dev/null)
    DIST_ID=$(aws cloudformation describe-stack-resources \
        --stack-name LlmGpuSizerStack \
        --region ap-southeast-7 \
        --query "StackResources[?ResourceType=='AWS::CloudFront::Distribution'].PhysicalResourceId" \
        --output text 2>/dev/null)
}

if [[ "$1" == "--quick" || "$1" == "-q" ]]; then
    echo -e "⚡ ${YELLOW}Quick deploy — frontend files only${NC}"
    echo ""

    get_stack_resources

    if [[ -z "$BUCKET" || -z "$DIST_ID" ]]; then
        echo -e "${RED}❌ Stack not found. Run full deploy first.${NC}"
        exit 1
    fi

    echo -e "📂 ${BLUE}Uploading files to S3...${NC}"
    aws s3 sync "$SCRIPT_DIR" "s3://$BUCKET" \
        --exclude "infrastructure/*" \
        --exclude "node_modules/*" \
        --exclude ".git/*" \
        --exclude "*.md" \
        --exclude "deploy.sh" \
        --region ap-southeast-7

    echo -e "🔄 ${BLUE}Invalidating CloudFront cache...${NC}"
    aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
        --distribution-id "$DIST_ID" \
        --paths "/*" \
        --query 'Invalidation.Id' \
        --output text

    echo ""
    echo -e "${GREEN}✅ Quick deploy complete!${NC} (files uploaded + cache invalidated)"
    echo -e "🌐 ${CYAN}https://$(aws cloudfront get-distribution --id "$DIST_ID" --query 'Distribution.DomainName' --output text)${NC}"
    echo ""
else
    echo -e "📦 ${BLUE}Full CDK deploy${NC}"
    echo ""

    cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/infrastructure"

    echo -e "📦 ${BLUE}Installing dependencies...${NC}"
    npm install --silent

    echo -e "🔨 ${BLUE}Synthesizing CDK stack...${NC}"
    npx cdk synth --quiet

    echo -e "🚀 ${YELLOW}Deploying to AWS...${NC}"
    npx cdk deploy --require-approval never

    echo ""
    echo -e "${GREEN}✅ Full deployment complete!${NC}"
    echo -e "🌐 ${CYAN}Your site is live at the CloudFront URL above.${NC}"
    echo ""
fi

echo -e "${PURPLE}Usage:${NC}"
echo -e "  ./deploy.sh          Full CDK deploy (infra + code)"
echo -e "  ./deploy.sh --quick  Frontend only (S3 sync + CF invalidation)"
echo ""

Done. The Break-Even Calculator now shows a **Memory Check** after results:

- **⚠️ Red warning** — if the instance doesn't have enough VRAM for the model (shows deficit in GB and suggests quantization or larger instance)
- **✅ Green OK** — if memory fits (shows headroom available)

It estimates VRAM needed using FP16 weights × 1.6 overhead factor, and for MoE models it uses ~25% active parameters. For example, selecting Llama 3.3 70B with a g5.xlarge (24 GB) will show a clear deficit warning.
